title: Similarity
blurb: Ratio, proportion and scale factor; similar polygons; the AA·SSS·SAS similarity shortcuts; the side-splitter theorem; perimeter and area ratios; and dilations with indirect measurement.
buildsOn: Triangle congruence and the angle sum (Unit 4), parallel-line angle facts (Unit 3), the midsegment (Unit 5), and ratios & proportions from Grade 6.

lessons.title: Ratio, Proportion & Scale Factor
lessons.concreteComparison: A map's scale, a recipe doubled for twice the guests, a die-cast model car — each shrinks or grows real sizes by the same **factor**. Nail that factor and the copy keeps the shape of the original; get it wrong and the model looks stretched. This is the engine behind everything in this unit.
lessons.objective: Write and simplify ratios, solve proportions using cross products, and use a scale factor to relate two sizes of the same shape.
concept: A **ratio** compares two quantities by division, written $a : b$ or $\tfrac{a}{b}$ (from Grade 6). A **proportion** states that two ratios are equal: $\tfrac{a}{b} = \tfrac{c}{d}$.
In a true proportion the **cross products are equal**: $\tfrac{a}{b} = \tfrac{c}{d}$ exactly when $a\,d = b\,c$. That lets you solve for a missing term.
A **scale factor** is the ratio of a new length to the matching original length ($\text{image} \div \text{original}$). Multiply every length by it to resize a shape.
A scale factor greater than $1$ **enlarges**, one between $0$ and $1$ **shrinks**, and exactly $1$ keeps the same size.
lessons.keyIdea: A proportion sets two ratios equal; its cross products are equal (ad = bc), which solves for a missing term. A scale factor (image ÷ original) resizes every length by the same multiple.

facts.title: Cross products
facts.explanation: Equal ratios have equal cross products.

facts.title: Scale factor
facts.explanation: Multiply each length by k to resize.

commonMistakes.text: Adding to compare instead of dividing.
commonMistakes.correction: A ratio compares by **division**. $6:4$ simplifies to $3:2$ (divide by 2), it doesn't become $2$ (subtract).

commonMistakes.text: Cross-multiplying across an equals sign the wrong way.
commonMistakes.correction: For $\tfrac{a}{b} = \tfrac{c}{d}$, multiply each numerator by the OTHER denominator: $a\,d = b\,c$.

steps.title: Same shape, resized
beats: A **ratio** compares two amounts by dividing: $6 : 4$ means $6$ for every $4$.
A **proportion** says two ratios are equal: $\tfrac{6}{4} = \tfrac{3}{2}$.
The number you multiply every length by to resize is the **scale factor**.
Keep the scale factor the same on every side and the shape is preserved.

steps.title: One factor resizes every side
steps.teach: The image is the original scaled by $k$. Step the scale factor up and down: every side is multiplied by the same $k$, and the angles never change. That single number IS the scale factor.

steps.title: Solving a proportion
beats: A proportion $\tfrac{a}{b} = \tfrac{c}{d}$ is true exactly when $a\,d = b\,c$.
To solve for a missing term, set the cross products equal.
$\tfrac{3}{4} = \tfrac{9}{x} \Rightarrow 3x = 36 \Rightarrow x = 12$.
Always multiply each numerator by the opposite denominator.

steps.title: Simplify ratios
steps.intro: Divide both parts by their common factor.

examples.prompt: Simplify $12 : 8$.
Simplify $9 : 15$.

steps.title: Solve proportions
steps.intro: Set the cross products equal, then solve.

examples.prompt: $\tfrac{3}{4} = \tfrac{9}{x}$. Find $x$.
$\tfrac{x}{6} = \tfrac{10}{15}$. Find $x$.

steps.title: The resizing number
beats: **Scale factor** $= \dfrac{\text{new length}}{\text{original length}}$.
From $6$ to $15$: scale factor $= \tfrac{15}{6} = \tfrac{5}{2} = 2.5$.
Bigger than $1$ enlarges; between $0$ and $1$ shrinks.
Multiply every original length by the scale factor to get the new one.

steps.title: Find and use a scale factor

examples.prompt: A side grows from $8$ to $12$. Scale factor?
Scale factor $3$. A side of $5$ becomes —

problems.prompt: Scale factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$: a side of $14$ becomes —
A scale factor of $1$ means the copy is —

steps.title: How map scales work
steps.body: A map scale like $1 : 100{,}000$ is just a ratio: $1$ cm on the map is $100{,}000$ cm ($1$ km) on the ground. To find a real distance you set up a proportion and cross-multiply — the exact skill in this lesson. Architects and machinists read scale drawings the same way.

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **proportion** sets two ratios equal; it holds exactly when the **cross products** are equal ($ad = bc$).
A **scale factor** $= \dfrac{\text{image}}{\text{original}}$ resizes every length by the same multiple.
$k > 1$ enlarges, $0 < k < 1$ shrinks, $k = 1$ keeps it congruent.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's simplify ratios, solve proportions, and work with scale factors.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

Scale factor $\tfrac{2}{3}$: a side of $9$ becomes —
A proportion $\tfrac{a}{b} = \tfrac{c}{d}$ is true exactly when —

steps.title: You've finished "Ratio, Proportion & Scale Factor"
steps.body: You can solve any proportion and read a scale factor. Next: what it means for two whole shapes to be similar — equal angles and proportional sides.

lessons.title: Similar Polygons
lessons.concreteComparison: A wallet photo and a poster of the same picture are **similar**: every angle is identical and every length is blown up by the same factor. Stretch only the width, though, and the face looks wrong — that's what breaks similarity. Similar means same shape, any size.
lessons.objective: Identify similar polygons (corresponding angles congruent and corresponding sides proportional), read a similarity statement, and use the scale factor to find a missing side.
concept: Two polygons are **similar** (written $\sim$) when **both** hold: their **corresponding angles are congruent**, and their **corresponding sides are proportional** (in the same ratio).

That common ratio is the **scale factor**. A **similarity statement** such as $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$ lists vertices in matching order, so $\dfrac{AB}{DE} = \dfrac{BC}{EF} = \dfrac{CA}{FD}$.
**Congruent** figures are just the special case of similar with scale factor $1$ (same shape AND same size).
To find a missing side, match it with its corresponding side and set up a **proportion**.
lessons.keyIdea: Similar polygons have congruent corresponding angles AND proportional corresponding sides (a common scale factor). The similarity statement lists vertices in matching order; use a proportion to find missing sides.

workedExamples.statement: $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$ with $AB = 6$, $DE = 9$. Find the scale factor from $ABC$ to $DEF$.

commonMistakes.text: Assuming equal angles alone make polygons similar.
commonMistakes.correction: For polygons with more than 3 sides you need **both** equal angles AND proportional sides. (A square and a non-square rectangle have equal angles but aren't similar.)

commonMistakes.text: Pairing sides in the wrong order.
commonMistakes.correction: Use the **similarity statement's order**: $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$ pairs $AB$ with $DE$, $BC$ with $EF$, and so on.

steps.title: Two things must match
beats: Two polygons are **similar** when they have the same **shape**.
That needs **two** things: all **corresponding angles equal**...
...and all **corresponding sides in the same ratio** (proportional).
The shared ratio is the **scale factor**.

steps.title: Angles fixed, sides in ratio
steps.teach: As you rescale, notice the two conditions of similarity at once: the marked corresponding angles stay equal, and every side keeps the same ratio $k$ to its partner. Same shape, any size.

steps.title: Order tells you the pairs
beats: $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$ pairs the vertices **in order**.
$A \leftrightarrow D$, $B \leftrightarrow E$, $C \leftrightarrow F$.
So $\dfrac{AB}{DE} = \dfrac{BC}{EF} = \dfrac{CA}{FD}$.
Match sides by their letters, then set up a proportion.

examples.prompt: $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$: $AB = 6$, $DE = 9$. Scale factor $ABC \to DEF$?
$AB = 5$, $DE = 15$. Scale factor $ABC \to DEF$?

problems.prompt: For similarity, corresponding angles must be —
For similarity, corresponding sides must be —

steps.title: Set up a proportion
beats: Similar sides share the **same ratio** $k$.
Match the known side with its partner, and the unknown with its partner.
Write $\dfrac{\text{side}_1}{\text{match}_1} = \dfrac{\text{side}_2}{\text{match}_2}$.
Cross-multiply and solve.

examples.prompt: $\tfrac{AB}{DE} = \tfrac{3}{5}$ and $AB = 3$, so $DE = 5$. If $BC = 6$, find $EF$.
$\tfrac{2}{3} = \tfrac{8}{DE}$. Find $DE$.

steps.title: Similar triangles measured the Earth
steps.body: Around 240 BC, Eratosthenes found Earth's circumference using similar triangles and shadows in two cities — no satellites, just proportions. His answer was within a few percent of the modern value. Similar figures turn a measurement you *can* reach into one you can't.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Similar** polygons: corresponding **angles congruent** AND corresponding **sides proportional**.
The **similarity statement** lists vertices in matching order, giving the side pairs.
Use a **proportion** from matched sides to find a missing length; congruent = scale factor $1$.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's find scale factors and missing sides in similar figures.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Similar polygons must have corresponding sides that are —
Congruent triangles are similar with scale factor —
In $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$, side $BC$ corresponds to —
problems.explanation: Match the middle letters: $B\leftrightarrow E$, $C\leftrightarrow F$.

steps.title: You've finished "Similar Polygons"
steps.body: You can spot similar figures and find missing sides. Next: the shortcuts — AA, SSS, and SAS — that prove two TRIANGLES similar without checking everything.

lessons.title: Proving Triangles Similar
lessons.concreteComparison: You don't need every measurement to know two triangles are the same shape. Just as SSS and SAS shortcut congruence, three quick shortcuts — AA, SSS, and SAS — settle similarity from just a clue or two.
lessons.objective: Use the AA, SSS, and SAS similarity shortcuts to decide whether two triangles are similar.
concept: **AA (Angle-Angle)**: if two angles of one triangle are congruent to two angles of another, the triangles are **similar**. (The third pair matches automatically by the $180^\circ$ angle sum.)
**SSS similarity**: if all three pairs of corresponding sides are in the **same ratio**, the triangles are similar.
**SAS similarity**: if two pairs of sides are in the same ratio and the **included angles** are congruent, the triangles are similar.
Similar is **not** the same as congruent: congruence needs equal sizes (scale factor $1$), while similarity allows any scale factor.
lessons.keyIdea: Three shortcuts prove triangles similar: AA (two equal angle pairs), SSS~ (three sides in the same ratio), and SAS~ (two sides in ratio with congruent included angles). Similarity allows any scale factor; congruence forces factor 1.

facts.title: AA
facts.explanation: The most-used shortcut — two matching angles is enough.

facts.title: SSS~ / SAS~
facts.explanation: Three sides proportional, or two sides proportional with an equal included angle.

workedExamples.statement: Two triangles have angle pairs $50^\circ, 60^\circ$ in common. Are they similar?
workedExamples.solution: Yes — two pairs of congruent angles means **AA** similarity. (The third angles are both $70^\circ$.)

workedExamples.statement: Triangle 1 has sides $3, 4, 5$; triangle 2 has $6, 8, 10$. Similar?
workedExamples.solution: Each ratio is $\tfrac{6}{3} = \tfrac{8}{4} = \tfrac{10}{5} = 2$. All equal → **SSS** similarity.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking you need all three angles for AA.
commonMistakes.correction: **Two** pairs are enough — the third is forced by the angle sum. That's why it's AA, not AAA.
Similar means same **shape**; congruent means same shape AND **size** (scale factor exactly $1$).

tryIt.statement: Two triangles share angles $40^\circ$ and $80^\circ$. Similar? By which rule?
Sides $2, 3, 4$ and $6, 9, 12$: are the ratios equal?

steps.title: Shortcuts for same-shape
beats: To prove two triangles **similar**, you rarely need all six measurements.
**AA**: two matching angles.
**SSS**: three sides in the same ratio.
**SAS**: two sides in ratio with the angle between them matching.

steps.title: Angles stay; that's why AA works
steps.teach: Rescale the triangle — the angles never change, only the size. So if two triangles already share two angles, they must be the same shape at some scale factor. That is the AA shortcut.

steps.title: Two angles is enough
beats: If two angles of one triangle equal two of another, the third pair is forced (angle sum $= 180^\circ$).
All three angles match → same shape → **similar**.
This is the most common way to prove similarity.
Look for parallel lines and shared angles — they hand you equal angles.

examples.prompt: Triangles share $50^\circ$ and $60^\circ$. Similar?
Both triangles are right-angled and share another angle of $35^\circ$. Similar?

steps.title: Is it AA?
steps.intro: Two equal angle pairs settle it.

problems.prompt: How many pairs of equal angles does AA need?
Two triangles each have a $90^\circ$ and a $45^\circ$ angle. Similar?

steps.title: Ratios instead of equal lengths
beats: **SSS~**: all three side ratios equal → similar.
**SAS~**: two side ratios equal AND the **included** angles equal → similar.
These mirror the congruence shortcuts, but use **equal ratios** instead of equal lengths.
Check every ratio reduces to the **same** number.

examples.prompt: Sides $3,4,5$ and $6,8,10$. Similar?
Two sides ratio $\tfrac{4}{6} = \tfrac{6}{9}$ with equal included angles. Similar?

problems.prompt: Ratios $\tfrac{3}{6}$ and $\tfrac{4}{8}$ with equal included angle. Rule?
problems.explanation: Two equal side ratios + included angle = SAS.
$\tfrac{4}{2}=2$ but $\tfrac{7}{4}<2$, so the ratios aren't all equal.

steps.title: Why every 3-4-5 triangle looks the same
steps.body: All triangles with sides in the ratio $3 : 4 : 5$ are similar by SSS — from a tiny drafting triangle to a builder's giant $30$-$40$-$50$ layout rope. That is why the $3$-$4$-$5$ trick reliably makes a right angle at any size: the shape is fixed by the ratios.

steps.title: Which rule?
steps.prompt: Two triangles have two pairs of congruent angles. They are similar by —
steps.explanation: Two matching angle pairs is the AA similarity shortcut.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **AA**: two pairs of congruent angles prove similarity (third is automatic).
**SSS~**: all three side ratios equal. **SAS~**: two side ratios equal with congruent **included** angles.
Similar allows any scale factor; **congruent** is the scale-factor-$1$ case.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's name the shortcut — AA, SSS, or SAS — that proves each pair similar.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: AA similarity needs how many equal angle pairs?
Two side ratios equal plus the included angle equal is —

problems.explanation: $\tfrac{4}{2}=2$ but $\tfrac{9}{5}<2$, so not all ratios match.
Angles are preserved; sizes may differ.

steps.title: You've finished "Proving Triangles Similar"
steps.body: You can prove similarity with AA, SSS, or SAS. Next: what happens when a line runs parallel to one side of a triangle — the side-splitter theorem, straight from AA.

lessons.title: The Triangle Proportionality Theorem
lessons.concreteComparison: Tie a rope across a triangular sail parallel to its bottom edge. Wherever you tie it, it cuts the two slanted sides into the SAME proportion — a third of the way up one side is a third of the way up the other. That is the side-splitter theorem, and it falls straight out of AA similarity.
lessons.objective: Use the Triangle Proportionality (Side-Splitter) Theorem — a line parallel to one side of a triangle divides the other two sides proportionally — together with its converse.
concept: **Triangle Proportionality Theorem**: if a line is parallel to one side of a triangle and crosses the other two sides, it divides those two sides **proportionally**: $\dfrac{AD}{DB} = \dfrac{AE}{EC}$.
Why it works: the small top triangle is **similar** to the whole triangle (shared angle at $A$ plus equal corresponding angles from the parallel line — **AA**), so their sides are proportional.
The **converse** holds too: if a line divides two sides of a triangle proportionally, then it is **parallel** to the third side.
The triangle **midsegment** (Unit 5) is the special case where the parallel line hits the **midpoints** — the split ratio is $1 : 1$.
lessons.keyIdea: A line parallel to one side of a triangle splits the other two sides in the same ratio (AD/DB = AE/EC), because it creates a smaller similar triangle by AA. The converse proves parallelism from a proportional split.

facts.title: Side-Splitter
facts.explanation: A parallel line cuts the two sides proportionally.

facts.title: Converse
facts.explanation: A proportional split forces the line parallel to the third side.

commonMistakes.text: Splitting the WHOLE side against a piece.
commonMistakes.correction: Match the pieces consistently: top piece to bottom piece, $\tfrac{AD}{DB} = \tfrac{AE}{EC}$ — don't mix $AD$ (a part) with $AC$ (the whole).

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting the line must be parallel.
commonMistakes.correction: The proportion only holds when the cutting line is **parallel** to the third side. No parallel, no guarantee.

tryIt.statement: A parallel line hits both midpoints. The split ratio is —

steps.title: A parallel cut splits proportionally
beats: Draw a line across a triangle **parallel** to one side.
It crosses the other two sides at points $D$ and $E$.
It cuts those two sides in the **same ratio**: $\tfrac{AD}{DB} = \tfrac{AE}{EC}$.
This works wherever the parallel line sits.

steps.title: Same ratio, wherever it sits
steps.teach: Slide the parallel line up and down. The two side-ratios $AD/DB$ and $AE/EC$ stay equal at every height — the green check never turns off. That's the Triangle Proportionality Theorem.

steps.title: Why the split is proportional
steps.teach: The parallel line makes a smaller triangle similar to the whole one (AA), and similar triangles have proportional sides.

rows.reason: Corresponding angles: $\overline{DE} \parallel \overline{BC}$ cut by the sides as transversals (Unit 3)
Corresponding sides of similar triangles are proportional
A property of proportions: subtracting the shared part converts whole-to-whole into part-to-part

steps.title: Find the missing piece
steps.intro: Set up AD/DB = AE/EC and solve.

examples.prompt: $DE \parallel BC$: $AD = 4$, $DB = 6$, $AE = 6$. Find $EC$.
$DE \parallel BC$: $AD = 6$, $DB = 4$, $AE = 9$. Find $EC$.

problems.prompt: The theorem needs the cutting line to be —

steps.title: Proportional means parallel
beats: Run the theorem backwards.
If a line splits two sides so that $\tfrac{AD}{DB} = \tfrac{AE}{EC}$...
...then that line **must be parallel** to the third side.
It's a quick way to prove two segments parallel.

examples.prompt: $AD = 4$, $DB = 8$, $AE = 3$, $EC = 6$. Is $DE \parallel BC$?
$AD = 2$, $DB = 5$, $AE = 4$, $EC = 10$. Parallel?

problems.prompt: A midsegment splits the sides in the ratio —

steps.title: Dividing a board into equal strips
steps.body: Woodworkers use the converse trick to split a board into equal widths without measuring: lay a ruler diagonally across it, mark equal spacings, and draw lines parallel to the end. The parallel lines cut the board proportionally — equal marks on the ruler give equal strips on the board.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Triangle Proportionality**: a line parallel to one side splits the other two **proportionally** ($\tfrac{AD}{DB} = \tfrac{AE}{EC}$).
It follows from **AA similarity** — the small triangle is similar to the whole.
The **converse** turns a proportional split into a proof of **parallel**; the midsegment is the $1:1$ case.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's find missing pieces and test for parallel lines.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: The Side-Splitter Theorem is proven using —
problems.explanation: The small triangle is similar to the whole by AA.

problems.prompt: A parallel line through the midpoints splits each side —
The converse says a proportional split makes the line —

steps.title: You've finished "The Triangle Proportionality Theorem"
steps.body: You can split sides proportionally and prove parallels. Next: what similarity does to a whole figure's perimeter and area — one scales by k, the other by k².

lessons.title: Perimeters & Areas of Similar Figures
lessons.concreteComparison: Double every side of a photo and the frame around it (perimeter) doubles — but the amount of ink to fill it (area) quadruples. Length grows once; area grows twice. Missing that difference is why a pizza twice the diameter is FOUR times the food.
lessons.objective: Use the fact that similar figures with scale factor k have perimeters in the ratio k and areas in the ratio k².
concept: For similar figures with **scale factor $k$**, the ratio of **perimeters** is $k$ — perimeter is a length, so it scales once.
The ratio of **areas** is $k^2$ — area is length $\times$ length, so it scales **twice**.
Backwards: if the **area ratio** is known, the scale factor is its **square root**. Area ratio $9 \Rightarrow k = \sqrt{9} = 3$.
Example: scale factor $k = 3$ means perimeter $\times 3$ and area $\times 9$.
lessons.keyIdea: Similar figures with scale factor k have perimeters in ratio k and areas in ratio k². To recover k from an area ratio, take the square root.

facts.title: Area ratio
facts.explanation: Area scales twice — the square of the scale factor.

workedExamples.statement: Two similar triangles have scale factor $3$. If the small perimeter is $12$, find the large perimeter.
Scale factor $2$; the small area is $5$. Find the large area.

commonMistakes.correction: Area scales by the **square** of the scale factor. Double the sides → **four** times the area, not double.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to square-root when going from area ratio to scale factor.
commonMistakes.correction: If areas are in ratio $25$, the scale factor is $\sqrt{25} = 5$, not $25$.

tryIt.statement: Scale factor $4$; small perimeter $10$. Large perimeter?
Scale factor $3$; small area $6$. Large area?

steps.title: Length once, area twice
beats: Similar figures differ by a **scale factor** $k$.
**Perimeter** is a length, so it scales by $k$.
**Area** is length $\times$ length, so it scales by $k \times k = k^2$.
Double the size → perimeter $\times 2$, area $\times 4$.

steps.title: Perimeter ×k, area ×k²
steps.teach: Step the scale factor. Watch the two readouts: the perimeter multiplies by $k$, but the area multiplies by $k^2$. At $k = 2$ the area is already $4$ times bigger.

steps.title: Two directions grow
beats: A shape scaled by $k$ gets $k$ times as **wide** and $k$ times as **tall**.
Area depends on both, so it grows by $k \times k = k^2$.
Perimeter follows just the outline — one direction of length — so it grows by $k$.
That's why $k = 3$ gives area $\times 9$.

examples.prompt: Scale factor $3$, small perimeter $12$. Large perimeter?
Scale factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$, large perimeter $20$. Small perimeter?

problems.prompt: Perimeters of similar figures are in the ratio —

examples.prompt: Scale factor $2$, small area $5$. Large area?
Scale factor $3$, small area $7$. Large area?

problems.prompt: Areas of similar figures are in the ratio —

steps.title: From area ratio to scale factor
beats: Given the **area ratio**, undo the square: take the **square root**.
Area ratio $9 \Rightarrow k = \sqrt{9} = 3$.
Area ratio $25 \Rightarrow k = \sqrt{25} = 5$.
Then the perimeter ratio is that same $k$.

examples.prompt: Similar figures have area ratio $16$. Scale factor?
Area ratio $9$; the perimeter ratio is —

steps.title: Why a giant can't be just a big human
steps.body: Scale a person up 10 times and their weight (which grows with volume, $k^3$) rises 1000-fold, but their bone cross-sections (an area, $k^2$) grow only 100-fold. The bones would be crushed. This square-cube reasoning — the same $k^2$ idea — is why elephants have thick legs and insects can be spindly.

steps.title: Area jump
steps.prompt: Two similar figures have scale factor $5$. The larger area is how many times the smaller?

steps.title: What you learned
points: Similar figures, scale factor $k$: **perimeter ratio $= k$**, **area ratio $= k^2$**.
Area scales **twice** because it is length $\times$ length.
To get $k$ from an area ratio, take the **square root**.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's scale perimeters by k, areas by k², and recover k from area ratios.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Scale factor $3$, small perimeter $8$. Large perimeter?
Scale factor $2$, small area $7$. Large area?
Scale factor $4$: area grows by a factor of —
Scale factor $\tfrac{1}{3}$: area shrinks to — of the original.
Perimeter ratio $6$ means the area ratio is —

steps.title: You've finished "Perimeters & Areas of Similar Figures"
steps.body: You can scale perimeter and area correctly. Last stop: dilations — the transformation that produces similar figures — and using similar triangles to measure the unreachable.

lessons.title: Dilations & Indirect Measurement
lessons.concreteComparison: A projector throws a small slide onto a big screen: every point moves out from the bulb by the same factor. That's a **dilation**. The same idea lets you find a flagpole's height from its shadow — measure what you can reach, then scale up with a proportion.
lessons.objective: Describe a dilation by its center and scale factor, and use similar triangles for indirect measurement (shadows and mirrors).
concept: A **dilation** resizes a figure from a fixed **center** by a **scale factor $k$**: each image point lies on the ray from the center, $k$ times as far.
$k > 1$ is an **enlargement**, $0 < k < 1$ a **reduction**. The image is **similar** to the original — angles preserved, each length $\times k$, and image sides **parallel** to the originals.
**Indirect measurement**: a tall object and its shadow form a triangle **similar** to a short object and its shadow (same sun angle), so a proportion finds the unknown height.
Example: a $6$-ft person casts a $4$-ft shadow while a flagpole casts a $20$-ft shadow. Then $\dfrac{6}{4} = \dfrac{h}{20} \Rightarrow h = 30$ ft.
lessons.keyIdea: A dilation enlarges (k>1) or reduces (0<k<1) a figure from a center, producing a similar image with lengths ×k. Similar triangles from shadows or mirrors let you measure heights indirectly with a proportion.

facts.title: Dilation
facts.latex: image $= k \times$ original from a center
facts.explanation: k > 1 enlarges, 0 < k < 1 reduces; the image is similar.

facts.title: Indirect measurement
facts.explanation: Similar triangles let a shadow reveal a height.

workedExamples.statement: A triangle is dilated from the origin by scale factor $2$. A side of length $5$ becomes —
A $6$-ft person casts a $4$-ft shadow; a nearby tree casts a $20$-ft shadow. Find the tree's height $h$.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking a dilation changes the shape's angles.
commonMistakes.correction: A dilation only changes **size**. Angles stay equal and the image is **similar** to the original.

commonMistakes.text: Setting up the shadow proportion inconsistently.
commonMistakes.correction: Keep it height-over-shadow on both sides: $\tfrac{\text{height}_1}{\text{shadow}_1} = \tfrac{\text{height}_2}{\text{shadow}_2}$.

tryIt.statement: Dilation scale factor $3$; a side of $4$ becomes —
A $5$-ft sign casts a $2$-ft shadow; a pole casts an $8$-ft shadow. Pole height?

steps.title: Resize from a center
beats: A **dilation** pushes every point out from (or in toward) a fixed **center**.
Each image point sits on the ray from the center, $k$ times as far.
$k > 1$ makes it bigger; $0 < k < 1$ makes it smaller.
The image is **similar** — same shape, sides $\times k$.

steps.title: Enlarge and reduce from O
steps.teach: Step the scale factor. Every vertex moves along its ray from the center $O$, landing $k$ times as far. At $k = 2$ the image is twice the size; at $k = 0.5$ it's half. Angles never change.

steps.title: Size changes, shape doesn't
beats: A dilation multiplies every length by $k$.
Angles stay the same, so the image is **similar** to the original.
Image sides come out **parallel** to their originals.
Scale factor $k$ is exactly the similarity scale factor from earlier lessons.

examples.prompt: Scale factor $2$: a side of $5$ becomes —
Scale factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$: a side of $14$ becomes —

problems.prompt: Scale factor $3$: a side of $4$ becomes —
A dilated image is always ___ the original.

steps.title: Reach the unreachable
beats: The sun casts shadows at the same angle for everything nearby.
So a tall object + its shadow is **similar** to a short object + its shadow.
Set up height-over-shadow on both sides: $\tfrac{h_1}{s_1} = \tfrac{h_2}{s_2}$.
Solve the proportion for the height you can't reach.

examples.prompt: A $6$-ft person: $4$-ft shadow. Tree: $20$-ft shadow. Tree height?
A $5$-ft sign: $3$-ft shadow. Pole: $12$-ft shadow. Pole height?

problems.prompt: $5$-ft sign, $2$-ft shadow; pole casts $8$-ft shadow. Pole height?
$4$-ft child, $3$-ft shadow; flag casts $12$-ft shadow. Flag height?
Shadow triangles are similar because the sun's angle is —
problems.explanation: Equal sun angle gives AA similarity.

steps.title: How Thales measured a pyramid
steps.body: Legend says the Greek mathematician Thales found the height of the Great Pyramid by waiting until his own shadow equalled his height — then the pyramid's shadow equalled ITS height. It's the shadow proportion with scale factor $1$, and it worked without climbing a single step.

steps.title: Shadow height
steps.prompt: A $6$-ft man casts an $8$-ft shadow while a tree casts a $40$-ft shadow. How tall is the tree?

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **dilation** resizes from a **center** by scale factor $k$ ($>1$ enlarge, $0<k<1$ reduce); the image is **similar**, sides $\times k$ and parallel.
**Indirect measurement** uses **similar triangles** from shadows or mirrors.
Set up **height/shadow = height/shadow** and solve the proportion.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's dilate lengths and measure heights with shadow proportions.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Scale factor $4$: a side of $3$ becomes —
$6$-ft person, $4$-ft shadow; pole casts $10$-ft shadow. Pole height?
A dilation's image is ___ to the original.
Scale factor $\tfrac{1}{4}$: a side of $20$ becomes —
$3$-ft post, $2$-ft shadow; building casts $30$-ft shadow. Building height?

steps.title: You've finished "Dilations & Indirect Measurement"
steps.body: You can dilate figures and measure the unreachable with similar triangles — the finale of Similarity. Next unit: right triangles and trigonometry, where similarity becomes the sine, cosine, and tangent.

practice.statement: Two similar figures have corresponding sides $6$ and $9$. Find the scale factor, then the side of the larger figure that corresponds to a side of $12$ in the smaller.
practice.solution: Scale factor $= \dfrac{9}{6} = \dfrac{3}{2}$. The corresponding side is $12 \cdot \dfrac{3}{2} = 18$.
Corresponding sides share one ratio: $\dfrac{AB}{DE} = \dfrac{BC}{EF} \Rightarrow \dfrac{4}{6} = \dfrac{10}{EF} \Rightarrow EF = \dfrac{6 \cdot 10}{4} = 15$.

practice.statement: One triangle has angles $40°$ and $60°$; another also has angles $40°$ and $60°$. Are the triangles similar? Which criterion?
practice.solution: Yes — AA. Two matching angles force the third to match too ($180 - 40 - 60 = 80°$ in both), and equal angles are exactly what similarity means.

practice.statement: $\overline{DE} \parallel \overline{BC}$ cuts sides $AB$ and $AC$ of $\triangle ABC$. If $AD = 6$, $DB = 9$, and $AE = 8$, find $EC$.
Two similar triangles have scale factor $\dfrac{3}{5}$. The smaller has perimeter $24$. Find the larger's perimeter.
practice.solution: Perimeters scale by the same factor as sides: $24 \div \dfrac{3}{5} = 24 \cdot \dfrac{5}{3} = 40$.

practice.statement: Two similar figures have scale factor $\dfrac{2}{3}$. The smaller has area $36$. Find the larger's area.
practice.solution: Areas scale by the SQUARE of the scale factor: ratio $= \left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \tfrac{4}{9}$, so the larger area is $36 \cdot \dfrac{9}{4} = 81$.

practice.statement: A $18$-dm-tall student casts a $24$-dm shadow while a tree casts a $144$-dm shadow. Find the tree's height (in dm).
practice.solution: Same sun angle ⟹ similar triangles: $\dfrac{18}{24} = \dfrac{h}{144} \Rightarrow h = \dfrac{18 \cdot 144}{24} = 108$ dm.

practice.statement: A dilation centered at the origin with scale factor $\dfrac{3}{2}$ sends $(4, -6)$ to which point?

testYourself.statement: Similar polygons have corresponding sides $8$ and $12$. A side of $18$ in the larger corresponds to what side in the smaller?
Two triangles have an equal included angle between sides of $4, 6$ and $6, 9$ respectively. Are they similar?
testYourself.solution: Yes — SAS similarity: $\dfrac{4}{6} = \dfrac{6}{9} = \dfrac{2}{3}$ and the included angles match.

testYourself.statement: A line parallel to one side splits the other two sides in the ratio $\dfrac{5}{7}$. If the upper piece of the second side is $x$ and the lower is $21$, find $x$.
A dilation centered at the origin sends $(3, 5)$ to $(12, 20)$. Find the scale factor.
Two similar figures have areas $25$ and $49$. Find the ratio of their corresponding sides.
testYourself.solution: Side ratio is the square root of the area ratio: $\sqrt{\dfrac{25}{49}} = \dfrac{5}{7}$.

testYourself.statement: A $\tfrac{3}{2}$-m stick casts a $2$-m shadow at the same moment a building casts a $12$-m shadow. Find the building's height.
